summary: Orthodox Judaism is the collective name for the modern Jewish movements that hold to the traditional understanding of the Torah, the Written Torah together with the Oral Torah, as divinely revealed and binding, and to halakha (Jewish law) as obligatory and not subject to reform. On its own self-understanding it is not a new movement but the continuation of the received Torah of Sinai; scholars, by contrast, describe 'Orthodoxy' as a modern term and a self-designation that took shape in the nineteenth century in reaction to Reform and to modernity. It is internally diverse, spanning Modern Orthodoxy (associated with Samson Raphael Hirsch's Torah im Derekh Eretz and later, in America, with Joseph B. Soloveitchik) and the Haredi or 'ultra-Orthodox' currents (the Lithuanian/yeshivish and the Hasidic), which differ over how far to engage secular culture.
description:
Orthodox Judaism is the term for the modern Jewish movements that affirm the traditional doctrine of the twofold Torah, the Written Torah (Torah she-bi-khtav) together with the Oral Torah (Torah she-be-al peh), as divinely revealed at Sinai and binding, and that hold halakha, the body of Jewish law, to be obligatory and not open to reform in accordance with the spirit of the age. Presented here on its own terms, Orthodoxy understands itself not as one modern denomination among others but as the faithful continuation of the received tradition, the Judaism of the rabbis and the codes carried forward unbroken; its adherents commonly describe it simply as Torah-true Judaism or Torah Judaism rather than by a party name. Samson Raphael Hirsch, in his Nineteen Letters (1836), argues that Judaism must be understood from itself, from the Written and Oral law it points to, and that the commandments retain their divine authority and cannot be selectively set aside or reshaped to suit emancipation and contemporary convenience.

In the scholarly account of its origins, presented here alongside the movement's own self-understanding rather than in place of it, 'Orthodoxy' is a modern coinage and a self-designation that crystallized in nineteenth-century Central Europe. Jacob Katz argued that Orthodoxy is a product of the passage into modernity: where in traditional society adherence to the law was simply the taken-for-granted fabric of Jewish life, under emancipation, Enlightenment, and the rise of the Reform movement that adherence became instead a conscious, defended choice, and it is this self-aware traditionalism, formed in reaction to the alternatives, that the historians call Orthodoxy. Adam Ferziger, studying the responsa and correspondence of German Orthodox rabbis from the Hatam Sofer to Hirsch, traces how the emergent Orthodox leadership remapped the internal boundaries of the Jewish community in a world where the observant had become a minority. On this reading the label is modern even though its bearers understand their content to be ancient; the two descriptions are held together here without ruling that either cancels the other.

Orthodoxy is internally diverse, and this entry notes its principal currents without ranking them. Modern Orthodoxy, in the German tradition of Hirsch's Torah im Derekh Eretz (Torah together with the way of the world), holds that strict observance of the law is fully compatible with, and enriched by, engagement with general culture, science, and civic life, and in its later American form, associated with Joseph B. Soloveitchik, it is often joined to religious Zionism; Soloveitchik's Halakhic Man presents the religious personality who lives wholly within the halakhic system. The Haredi or 'ultra-Orthodox' currents, comprising the Lithuanian or 'yeshivish' world centred on Talmud study and the Hasidic communities gathered around their rebbes, place greater emphasis on separation from secular culture and minimize accommodation to it. These currents share the affirmation of the divinely given and binding twofold Torah and of halakha as obligatory; they differ over the degree and manner of engagement with modernity, and the entry records that difference as a matter internal to Orthodoxy rather than as a hierarchy. Where Orthodoxy differs from Conservative and Reform Judaism over the authority and mutability of halakha, those differences are stated on each movement's own terms in the related links below, and this entry does not rule which movement is the authentic or correct Judaism.
editorial_notes: This entry presents Orthodox Judaism on its own terms (neutrality #1, #4, #6, #7), recording as its attributed self-understanding the claim to be the faithful continuation of the received Torah of Sinai (the divinely given and binding twofold Torah, halakha obligatory and not subject to reform), stated as the movement's position rather than affirmed or denied by the editor. Per neutrality #2 the scholarly account (Katz, Ferziger) that 'Orthodoxy' is a modern term and self-designation formed in reaction to Reform and modernity is presented ALONGSIDE that self-understanding, not as a verdict against it; the two are held together and neither is ruled to cancel the other. The internal diversity of Orthodoxy (Modern Orthodoxy / Torah im Derekh Eretz and the Haredi Lithuanian and Hasidic currents) is noted without ranking. The differences among Orthodox, Conservative, and Reform over the authority and mutability of halakha are attributed to each movement (see related_to links and the halakha entry, which already attributes Soloveitchik for the Orthodox view from within and Dorff for the Conservative), and the editor does not rule which is the authentic Judaism (THE crux, neutrality #1-#2). Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality #8-9: academic historians of the emergence of Orthodoxy (Jacob Katz, 'Orthodoxy in Historical Perspective'; Adam Ferziger, Exclusion and Hierarchy); a primary text read openly (Samson Raphael Hirsch, The Nineteen Letters, Drachman translation of 1899, public domain and openly readable on the Internet Archive, read in excerpt for the load-bearing claims that Judaism is to be understood from the Written and Oral law and that the commandments are not to be selectively set aside or reshaped to the spirit of the age); and a contemporary Jewish scholar's voice within the movement (Soloveitchik, Halakhic Man, already cited in the halakha entry). No encyclopedias (including the Encyclopaedia Judaica, the Jewish Encyclopedia, Chabad.org, or My Jewish Learning), no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources. Confidence: the Nineteen Letters, read openly in the public-domain Drachman translation for the specific claims cited, is high (verified-eligible); Katz, Ferziger, and Soloveitchik are verified to exist and be on-topic (author/title/publisher/year confirmed) but not read line-by-line, so medium. The entry rests its verified tier on the openly-read Hirsch primary for the specific load-bearing claims cited, with the secondary works held at medium. Relation targets rabbinic-judaism, oral-torah, torah, and halakha exist; reform-judaism and conservative-judaism are authored in the same batch; samson-raphael-hirsch and joseph-soloveitchik are dangling forward references to not-yet-created figures.

period.note: Orthodoxy understands its content as the received Torah of Sinai, so from within the movement no founding date applies. In the scholarly account followed here alongside that self-understanding, 'Orthodoxy' as a self-aware movement and self-designation took shape from the late eighteenth century onward and crystallized in nineteenth-century Central Europe in reaction to emancipation, Enlightenment, and the Reform movement (Katz); the approximate late-eighteenth-century marker is a conventional scholarly starting point for that self-aware traditionalism, not a claim that Orthodox belief or practice began then. Orthodox Judaism remains active and diverse to the present, so no end date is given.

relations.note: Samson Raphael Hirsch (1808-1888) is the classic exponent of the Modern Orthodox current, whose Torah im Derekh Eretz combines strict observance with engagement in general culture; his Nineteen Letters (1836) is a foundational statement of the Orthodox position against Reform. 'Founded_by' is used for his role as a formative figure of the movement's modern articulation, not a claim that he originated Judaism. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created figure.

Joseph B. Soloveitchik (1903-1993) is the leading figure of American Modern Orthodoxy in the twentieth century; his Halakhic Man presents the religious personality who lives wholly within the halakhic system. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created figure.

Orthodox Judaism holds, on its own self-understanding, that the Oral Torah (Torah she-be-al peh) was given at Sinai together with the Written Torah and is divinely authoritative, and that halakha derived from it is binding.

Orthodox Judaism holds halakha (Jewish law) to be divinely grounded, obligatory, and not subject to reform according to the spirit of the age; the halakha entry attributes this position (via Soloveitchik) alongside the differing Conservative and Reform positions without adjudicating among them.
Orthodoxy centres on the Torah understood as the twofold Torah, the Written Torah together with the Oral Torah, held to have been divinely revealed at Sinai and to be binding.

Orthodox Judaism understands itself as the faithful continuation of rabbinic Judaism, the Judaism of the Mishnah, the Talmuds, and the codes, carried forward without reform; this is stated as the movement's self-understanding.

Orthodox and Conservative (Masorti) Judaism differ over halakha: Orthodoxy holds it divinely given and not subject to reform, while Conservative Judaism holds it binding yet historically developing and open to change through its own rabbinic authority. Each position is attributed to its movement; this entry does not rule which is the authentic Judaism (neutrality #1-#2). Authored in the same batch.

Orthodoxy took shape in part in reaction to Reform Judaism and differs from it sharply over the binding authority of the ritual law: Orthodoxy holds the full halakha divinely given and obligatory, whereas classical Reform treats much of the ritual law as non-binding. Each position is attributed to its movement; the editor does not rule which is authentic or correct (neutrality #1-#2). Authored in the same batch.

sources.citation: Hirsch, Samson Raphael. The Nineteen Letters of Ben Uziel: Being a Spiritual Presentation of the Principles of Judaism. Trans. Bernard Drachman. New York: Funk and Wagnalls, 1899 (German original, Neunzehn Briefe über Judentum, 1836). Primary text of Modern Orthodoxy; cited and read openly in excerpt for Hirsch's insistence that Judaism is to be understood from the Written and Oral law it points to (Second Letter), that the commandments retain their divine authority and are not to be selectively set aside or reshaped to emancipation and the spirit of the age (Sixteenth to Eighteenth Letters), and for the programme of observance combined with engagement in general culture (Torah im Derekh Eretz). Cited as the primary Orthodox source on its own terms, not as a neutral reference work.
sources.license: Drachman translation (1899) in the public domain (not in copyright); openly readable and searchable on the Internet Archive. The cited passages read in excerpt for this entry.

sources.citation: Katz, Jacob. 'Orthodoxy in Historical Perspective.' In Peter Y. Medding, ed., Studies in Contemporary Jewry, vol. 2: The Challenge of Modernity and Jewish Orthodoxy. Bloomington: Indiana University Press, 1986, 3-17. Foundational scholarly essay by the leading social historian of the emergence of Orthodoxy; cited for the argument that 'Orthodoxy' is a modern phenomenon and self-designation, self-aware traditionalism formed in the passage into modernity and in reaction to the Reform movement, in which loyalty to the law became a conscious choice. Verified to exist and be on-topic (author, title, editor, volume, publisher, year, and pagination confirmed); not read line-by-line.

Ferziger, Adam S. Exclusion and Hierarchy: Orthodoxy, Nonobservance, and the Emergence of Modern Jewish Identity. Jewish Culture and Contexts. Philadelphia: University of Pennsylvania Press, 2005. Academic monograph on the emergence of Orthodoxy as a response to nonobservance in nineteenth-century Germany, drawing on the responsa and correspondence of German Orthodox rabbis from the Hatam Sofer to Samson Raphael Hirsch; cited for the account of how the emergent Orthodox leadership remapped the internal boundaries of the Jewish community as the observant became a minority. Verified to exist and be on-topic (author, title, series, publisher, and year confirmed); not read line-by-line.

Soloveitchik, Joseph B. Halakhic Man. Trans. Lawrence Kaplan. Philadelphia: Jewish Publication Society of America, 1983 (Hebrew, Ish ha-Halakhah, 1944). A classic work of modern Orthodox Jewish thought presenting the religious personality and worldview of one who lives within the halakhic system; cited for the Orthodox (Modern Orthodox) understanding of the halakhic life from within, as an attributed position and a contemporary Jewish scholar's voice. Verified to exist and be on-topic (author, title, translator, publisher, and year confirmed); not read line-by-line. Carries the same medium rating it holds in the halakha entry.
sources.license: Proprietary translation; cited for reference (publisher page).
